station.title: Occupational hand dermatitis
station.candidate_instructions:
Shown to the candidate during the three minute reading window. In this tool the consultation is run as audio (Part 1, Section 1.1), even though this case was authored as a video consultation, so the engine must not expect a live visual examination.

- Patient: Sophie Miller, 29.
- Past medical history: mild seasonal hay fever. No known history of asthma or childhood eczema.
- Medication: cetirizine 10mg once daily as required; combined oral contraceptive pill (Lucette); no known drug allergies.
- Recent notes: three months ago, presented with dry, itchy patches on the webs of her fingers, advised to use over the counter E45 cream.
- Reason for encounter: hands getting worse, affecting her work as a beautician.
station.station_script:
**Character overview.** Sophie is a self employed beautician specialising in acrylic nails and eyelash extensions. She is highly stressed because her hands are her livelihood. She is worried that she might be allergic to her job and is looking for a quick fix so she can keep working.

**Opening line.** "Hi Doctor, I'm really worried about my hands. I've had this rash for a few months, but now the skin is cracking and it's so painful I can't even apply treatments to my clients properly."

**History if asked (data gathering phase).**
- The rash: primarily on the fingertips and the webs of the fingers on both hands. Red, scaly, and occasionally develops tiny, itchy blisters.
- Occupational links: definitely worse after a long day at the salon. She uses acrylic monomers, nail glues, and various eyelash adhesives. Even through gloves her hands feel hot and itchy.
- Gloves: wears latex free nitrile gloves, but often takes them off to do fine detail work because they get in the way.
- Home life: has a six month old baby, so she is washing her hands constantly and doing lots of wet work (bottles, bathing) at home too.
- Impact: has had to cancel three high paying bridal bookings this week. Terrified this is permanent.

**ICE.** The patient does not volunteer these unprompted; they surface only if the candidate explores her perspective.
- Ideas: thinks she might be allergic to the chemicals she uses at work, the acrylic monomers or nail glues specifically, since it worsens after long salon days. Part of her wonders whether constant hand washing with the baby is making it worse, but she mainly blames the salon products.
- Concerns: biggest fear is that this is permanent and she will have to give up her career, which she has built over years. Also worried about money: self employed with a mortgage and a baby, every cancelled booking is lost income. A quieter worry too: she feels like a bad mum because her cracked hands make her wince when she bathes the baby.
- Expectations: wants something to clear it up quickly so she can get back to work, ideally a strong cream. Also wants a clear answer about whether it is the chemicals, because if she knew exactly which product was causing it she could just stop using that one.

**Medical history and medications if asked.**
- Hay fever and cetirizine: gets hay fever in summer, takes cetirizine when bad, more seasonal. Wondered if cetirizine might help the hand itching but it makes no difference.
- No history of eczema or asthma: never had eczema, not as a child, and no asthma.
- Contraceptive pill (Lucette): on it for years with no problems.
- Previous GP visit three months ago: advised to try E45, used it for a few weeks but it did not really help, felt okay for about ten minutes then her hands dried out again, stopped using it.
- Allergies: no known medicine allergies.

**Social history and lifestyle impact.** Weave naturally into conversation, not as a monologue.
- Work: runs her own mobile beauty business, travels to clients' homes so she cannot easily control the environment, works five or six days a week, three to four clients back to back. Built it from scratch before the baby; it is her sole income.
- Lifestyle impact: cracking on the fingertips means she physically cannot do fine detail work, which requires precise finger contact, and the pain is too much. Cancelled three bridal bookings this week alone, roughly 600 pounds, and regular clients are starting to go elsewhere. At home, bathing the baby stings badly so she asks her partner to do it, which makes her feel guilty. Getting dressed is uncomfortable as buttons and jeans catch on the cracks.

**Associated symptoms if asked.**
- Itching: itches a lot, especially the little blisters, hard not to scratch at night.
- Pain or burning: the cracks sting, especially with hand washing or sanitiser, more a burning pain than an ache.
- Rash elsewhere: no, just the hands.
- Face or eyelid involvement: no.
- Nail changes: no, the nails themselves look normal, it is just the skin around them.
- Joint pain or swelling: no.
- Fever or feeling unwell: no, feels fine in herself.
- Recent infections or illness: no.
- New products at home: no, same washing up liquid and soap, only suspicious of the salon products.
- Clears on days off or holidays: gets a bit better with a couple of days off but never fully goes; was better over a week off at Christmas but came straight back on return to work.
- Stress: yes she is stressed with a baby, a business, and now her hands, but does not think stress is causing it.
- Smoking: no.
- Alcohol: the odd glass of wine at the weekend.
- Family history: mum has asthma, no known family history of eczema.

**Management negotiation reactions.**
- If the doctor suggests stopping work: distressed. Self employed, no work means no pay, has a mortgage and a baby, surely there is a cream to clear it up.
- If the doctor suggests patch testing: hopeful but impatient. Will it tell her exactly which chemical it is, how long does the referral take, she needs an answer now.
- If the doctor mentions irritant versus allergic dermatitis: "I thought it was just the soap at work, but even when I use my own stuff, it's still there. Does that mean it's an allergy?" This tests the doctor's ability to explain the difference.
- If the doctor suggests soap substitutes: sceptical. How can she get her hands clean without soap when she has to be hygienic for clients.
station.data_gathering:
**Occupational history.** Positive: explores the specific chemicals used in the patient's work (acrylic monomers, nail glues, eyelash adhesives) and whether symptoms worsen after work shifts or improve on days off and holidays. Negative: fails to enquire about specific materials used at work or does not connect the occupation to the skin presentation.

**Glove use and skin protection.** Positive: asks about current glove practice, the type used, whether they are removed for fine detail work, and duration of wear during a typical day. Negative: does not ask about glove use or assumes gloves provide adequate protection without exploring actual practice.

**Wet work exposure.** Positive: identifies the cumulative irritant burden from both occupational chemicals and domestic wet work (frequent hand washing, bathing the baby, bottles). Negative: focuses only on salon chemicals and fails to ask about hand washing frequency or domestic wet work.

**Pattern and distribution.** Positive: establishes the distribution (fingertips, finger webs, bilateral), morphology (erythema, scaling, vesicles, fissuring), and chronology to support a working diagnosis. Negative: takes a vague or superficial history without establishing distribution, morphology, or time course.

**Temporal relationship.** Positive: asks whether the rash improves away from work to help distinguish allergic from irritant contact dermatitis. Negative: does not explore whether symptoms fluctuate with work patterns, missing a key diagnostic clue.

**Differential diagnosis, ACD versus ICD.** Positive: demonstrates structured reasoning by considering both allergic contact dermatitis (sensitisation to acrylates) and irritant contact dermatitis (cumulative wet work and chemical exposure), recognising they may coexist. Negative: assumes simple dry skin or generic eczema without an occupational aetiology, or labels it as one type without reasoning through the differential.

**Atopic background.** Positive: notes the personal history of hay fever and family history of maternal asthma as relevant, since atopic individuals have a lower threshold for irritant contact dermatitis. Negative: ignores the atopic history or fails to recognise its relevance.

**Previous treatment response.** Positive: establishes that the previously advised E45 was ineffective, providing only transient relief, to guide escalation. Negative: does not review the outcome of previous treatment or assumes the earlier advice was adequate.

**Red flag exclusion.** Positive: asks appropriately about features suggesting an alternative or more serious diagnosis, rash elsewhere, nail changes, joint symptoms, signs of secondary infection, or systemic unwellness. Negative: fails to exclude red flags or alternative diagnoses, or does not check for secondary infection in cracked skin.

**Impact assessment.** Positive: explores the functional, financial, and psychological impact, cancelled bookings, lost income, fear of career loss, guilt about being unable to bathe her baby, and the stress of business and a new baby. Negative: ignores the self employed status and financial pressures, or fails to explore the emotional and functional impact.
station.clinical_management:
**Explanation of diagnosis.** Positive: clearly explains the working diagnosis of contact dermatitis, distinguishing irritant from allergic mechanisms and that both may contribute, in language the patient understands. Negative: gives a vague or inaccurate label without explaining the occupational link, or fails to explain the irritant versus allergic difference when asked.

**Emollient therapy, soap substitutes.** Positive: recommends replacing soap and sanitiser with a soap substitute (for example Dermol 500, Cetraben wash, emulsifying ointment) and explains that soap strips the skin barrier and worsens irritant dermatitis, addressing the hygiene concern. Negative: does not recommend soap substitutes, or fails to address the scepticism about hand hygiene.

**Emollient therapy, leave on moisturiser.** Positive: recommends a greasy emollient (for example Diprobase ointment, white soft paraffin, Epaderm ointment) applied frequently and after hand washing, as ongoing maintenance not just during flares. Negative: does not recommend a leave on emollient, or recommends only the previously ineffective E45 without escalating.

**Potent topical corticosteroid.** Positive: prescribes a potent topical corticosteroid (for example betamethasone valerate 0.1 percent or mometasone furoate 0.1 percent) for the acute flare, appropriate to the thick skin of the hands. Negative: prescribes a mild corticosteroid (for example hydrocortisone 1 percent) which is inadequate for hand dermatitis, or avoids a topical steroid altogether due to unfounded safety concerns.

**Steroid prescribing detail.** Positive: gives clear, safe prescribing guidance, how much (fingertip unit), where, how often, and for how long (a defined course, for example two weeks then review), with advice to step down once settled. Negative: prescribes a potent steroid without specifying duration, frequency, or quantity, leaving the patient at risk of prolonged unsupervised use.

**Steroid safety counselling.** Positive: briefly explains the risk of skin thinning with prolonged use but reassures that a short supervised course on the hands is safe and necessary, balancing caution with effective treatment. Negative: provides no safety information, or over emphasises side effects to the point the patient avoids effective use.

**Skin protection strategy, glove technique.** Positive: advises improved glove technique, cotton liner gloves under nitrile to reduce chemical penetration and sweat irritation, and keeping gloves on for chemical contact rather than removing them for detail work, explored collaboratively as a work modification. Negative: advises the patient to stop working immediately without exploring modifications, or does not discuss glove technique at all.

**Barrier cream.** Positive: considers a barrier cream before glove use or wet work as an additional protective measure. Negative: does not mention barrier creams (a minor omission if other protection advice is comprehensive).

**Patch testing referral.** Positive: recognises that occupational exposure to acrylates and adhesives with a pattern suggestive of allergic contact dermatitis requires referral to dermatology for patch testing, and explains what it involves and why. Negative: tells the patient there is no way to find out which chemical is causing it, or fails to arrange or discuss patch testing when the history strongly suggests allergic contact dermatitis.

**Manages referral expectations.** Positive: acknowledges the referral may take time and sets realistic expectations, while explaining the interim plan will manage symptoms meanwhile. Negative: offers referral without any interim plan, or dismisses her impatience about timescales.

**Wet work reduction.** Positive: advises reducing domestic wet work, cotton lined rubber gloves for washing up and bathing the baby, drying hands thoroughly, and reapplying emollient after each wash. Negative: does not address the domestic wet work burden.

**Safety netting, secondary infection.** Positive: advises watching for signs of secondary bacterial infection in cracked skin (increasing pain, spreading redness, weeping, pus, warmth, feeling unwell) and to seek review promptly. Negative: does not safety net for secondary infection despite cracked, fissured skin.

**Safety netting, treatment non response.** Positive: gives clear guidance on when to return, if the rash does not improve after the steroid course, if it worsens, or if new symptoms develop, and ensures the patient knows the plan is iterative. Negative: does not arrange or suggest follow up, leaving no plan if treatment fails.

**Follow up plan.** Positive: arranges a specific follow up (for example review in two to three weeks) to assess response, check steroid use, and chase the referral, demonstrating continuity. Negative: provides no follow up arrangement, or leaves it entirely to the patient.

**Holistic and multi morbidity awareness.** Positive: recognises the intersection of the dermatitis with self employment without sick pay, new motherhood, financial pressure, and stress, and factors this into the approach. Negative: treats the skin in isolation without acknowledging the wider psychosocial and financial context.

**Occupational health considerations.** Positive: considers signposting to occupational health or the professional body for workplace adaptations for self employed beauticians, or mentions occupational dermatitis may be reportable. Negative: does not consider occupational health resources (a minor omission if other management is comprehensive).
station.relating_to_others:
**Empathy for livelihood and identity.** Positive: shows genuine understanding that her hands are central to her career and identity, acknowledging the fear of losing a career built from scratch, the financial pressure, and the emotional weight of self employment with a baby and a mortgage. Negative: dismissive of financial concerns or treats the occupational impact as secondary.

**Exploring ICE, ideas.** Positive: actively explores her own idea that the salon chemicals are the cause, and uses it as a starting point rather than overriding it. Negative: does not ask what she thinks is causing it, or dismisses her theory.

**Exploring ICE, concerns.** Positive: elicits the deeper concerns, the fear it is permanent, the worry about financial survival, and the quieter guilt about being unable to bathe her baby. Negative: fails to explore concerns beyond the surface complaint.

**Exploring ICE, expectations.** Positive: establishes what she is hoping for, a quick fix to get back to work and a clear answer about which chemical is responsible, and addresses these honestly. Negative: does not ask what she is hoping for, leading to a plan that does not align with her priorities.

**Responding to emotional cues.** Positive: picks up and responds to significant emotional cues, distress about bookings, guilt about the baby, anxiety about the future. Negative: ignores or fails to respond to emotional cues, staying purely biomedical.

**Collaborative problem solving.** Positive: works in partnership to find practical work modifications rather than dictating avoidance, presenting options and negotiating a mutually acceptable plan. Negative: uses a paternalistic or directive approach, telling her to stop working without alternatives.

**Addressing scepticism constructively.** Positive: when she is sceptical about soap substitutes, responds with a clear, non condescending explanation that addresses her hygiene concern. Negative: responds dismissively or fails to explain the rationale, leaving her unlikely to follow the advice.

**Sensitive discussion of work modification.** Positive: discusses reducing chemical and irritant exposure sensitively, as temporary measures and adaptations rather than career ending advice. Negative: bluntly tells her to stop working or find another job, or uses a patronising tone.

**Managing uncertainty honestly.** Positive: when she wants a definitive answer about the chemical, explains honestly that patch testing is needed, managing her frustration with empathy rather than giving false certainty. Negative: guesses the specific chemical without evidence, or dismisses her need for a clear answer.

**Acknowledging the parenting impact.** Positive: recognises and validates her guilt about her hands affecting her ability to care for her baby, without judgement, and offers practical advice. Negative: fails to acknowledge the impact on her role as a mother.

**Checking understanding.** Positive: checks she understands the plan, what each treatment is for, how to use the steroid, when to return, and what the referral is for. Negative: does not check understanding, or overloads her without confirming she has grasped the key points.

**Maintaining hope and continuity.** Positive: provides realistic reassurance that the condition is treatable and a clear plan is in place, without minimising severity or making promises that cannot be kept. Negative: gives false reassurance, or is overly pessimistic, leaving her feeling hopeless.
station.clinical_learning_points:
The clinical model answer, authored to be NICE and SIGN concordant. This is the primary clinical source of truth for the engine on this case.

**Diagnostic distinction, ICD versus ACD.**
- Irritant contact dermatitis results from cumulative physical and chemical damage to the skin barrier, repeated wet work, soap, and chemical irritants causing inflammation without immune sensitisation. It is the more common of the two in Sophie's situation.
- Allergic contact dermatitis is a Type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction requiring prior sensitisation. Once sensitised, even trace exposures trigger a response, which is why symptoms can occur through gloves or on lower exposure days.
- In beauticians the principal allergens are acrylates (acrylic monomer and UV gel products) and cyanoacrylates (nail and lash adhesives), among the most common occupational allergens in this profession.
- The two frequently coexist: ICD damages the barrier and lowers the threshold for sensitisation. Treating them as mutually exclusive is a common diagnostic error.
- A key distinguishing clue is the temporal pattern: ICD correlates with total exposure burden (worse after long shifts, better on sustained rest); ACD may flare with brief contact once sensitised. Improvement over Christmas with rapid relapse on return supports an occupational aetiology for both.
- Atopic individuals (hay fever or family history of asthma) have a lower threshold for ICD. Sophie's atopic background is clinically relevant.

**Investigations, patch testing.**
- Patch testing is the gold standard for suspected ACD and is essential when occupational sensitisation is suspected. It cannot be performed in primary care.
- Refer to dermatology when occupational allergy is suspected, the diagnosis is uncertain, or dermatitis is not responding adequately.
- The occupational series includes acrylate and adhesive/cyanoacrylate series, which are not part of the standard baseline series and must be specifically requested.
- Patch testing should not be performed during an acute flare or while using potent topical steroids; timing of referral and treatment should account for this.
- Advise Sophie to check Safety Data Sheets for her products to share with the dermatologist.

**Acute flare management.**
- Soap substitutes: replace all soap and sanitiser with a soap substitute for every wash. Soap is a significant irritant. Soap substitutes are effective cleansers and do not compromise hygiene, which must be explained explicitly.
- Leave on emollient: prescribe a greasy emollient applied frequently, after every wash and at bedtime. Ointments are more effective than lotions for hand dermatitis; E45 cream is insufficient for an acute flare with fissuring.
- Topical corticosteroid potency: the thick skin of the hands requires a potent topical corticosteroid (betamethasone valerate 0.1 percent or mometasone furoate 0.1 percent). Hydrocortisone 1 percent is inadequate and is one of the most common prescribing errors in hand dermatitis.
- Regimen: apply once or twice daily to affected areas for a defined course, typically two weeks, then review, with fingertip unit guidance.
- Steroid safety counselling: acknowledge skin thinning with prolonged use, but reassure that a supervised two week course on the hands is safe and necessary. Over emphasising side effects leads to under treatment.

**Skin protection strategy.**
- Glove technique: nitrile gloves are necessary but insufficient if removed for fine work. Keep gloves on for chemical contact, including detail work. Consider thinner nitrile gloves for tactile sensitivity.
- Cotton liner gloves: cotton gloves under nitrile reduce sweat accumulation and chemical penetration through micro perforations; trapped sweat is itself an irritant.
- Domestic wet work reduction: cotton lined rubber gloves for washing up and bathing the baby, dry hands thoroughly, reapply emollient immediately.
- Barrier creams: a silicone based barrier cream before glove use or wet work is a useful adjunct, not a replacement; evidence is weaker than for emollients.
- Latex avoidance: confirm gloves are latex free; latex contact urticaria is a separate occupational risk.

**Referral criteria.**
- Refer to dermatology for patch testing when occupational ACD is suspected, which applies to Sophie.
- Also refer when the diagnosis is uncertain, the condition is not responding after an adequate trial, or there are atypical features.
- Occupational health referral or signposting to the professional body may assist with workplace adaptations.
- Confirmed occupational dermatitis may be reportable and relevant to industrial injury benefit, worth flagging even for self employed workers if subcontracting arrangements exist.

**Safety netting and follow up.**
- Secondary infection: fissured skin is a portal of entry. Advise watching for spreading redness, increasing pain, warmth, weeping, purulent discharge, or systemic unwellness, and to seek urgent review. Eczema herpeticum is rare but serious and needs same day assessment.
- Review at two to three weeks to assess response, check emollient use, and chase the referral. Do not leave follow up entirely to the patient.
- Non response after a two week course: consider secondary infection, poor adherence, wrong diagnosis, or short course oral steroid, and discuss with dermatology.
- Provide a written plan where possible, given her high cognitive load.

**Common candidate mistakes.**
- Prescribing a mild steroid for hand dermatitis is the most common error in this case. Potent steroids are required for the thickened, fissured skin of the hands.
- Failing to recommend soap substitutes, or recommending them without addressing the hygiene concern, results in a plan the patient will not follow.
- Omitting emollient escalation: recommending E45 when a greasy ointment is required is inadequate escalation.
- Not discussing domestic wet work: the frequent hand washing and baby care at home contribute as much as the occupational chemicals.
- Telling the patient to stop working without exploring alternatives: workplace modification, not cessation, is the appropriate first step.
- Omitting patch testing referral: without it, Sophie leaves with no route to a definitive diagnosis.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Hello, am I speaking with Sophie? Thanks for waiting. Tell me what is going on with your hands.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Hi doctor, yes. I have had this rash for a few months but now the skin is cracking and it is so painful I cannot apply treatments to my clients properly.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: You mentioned clients. What is it you do, and when are the hands at their worst?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: I am a mobile beautician, nails and lashes. It is worse after a long day at the salon, my hands feel hot and itchy even through the gloves.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Is there ever a time it settles, for example when you are away from work?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: It was a bit better over Christmas when I had a week off, but it came straight back when I went back.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Any history of hay fever, asthma or eczema, in you or the family?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Hay fever in summer, I take cetirizine sometimes. No eczema ever. My mum has asthma.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: What is worrying you most about all this?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Honestly, that it is permanent and I will have to give up my career. I built this from nothing and I have a mortgage and a baby.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: And I feel awful, I wince when I bathe my little one, my partner has to do it now.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Okay. Let me ask a few more things about the rash itself.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: So I think this is a contact dermatitis, your skin reacting to things it touches.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Is it the chemicals then, an allergy? Because even with my own stuff at home it is there.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: It could be, it is hard to say exactly.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: I will prescribe hydrocortisone one percent cream to settle it, and keep using a moisturiser like E45.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Come back if it is not improving.
